← the Atlas

kolu-tui — a real TUI version of kolu, remote-first

Features·seedling·proposed·JUN 30, 2026

Not another raw single-daemon CLI — kolu-tui is kolu itself in the terminal, in kolu's own two-pane shape: the DOCK rail on the left (the terminal fleet you switch between), the live terminal you type into as the main panel. It EMBEDS @kolu/sutra (own process, own state — Option B, not a thin renderer of a running kolu-server) and renders that layout with OpenTUI (the SolidJS-reconciler terminal framework, the lib that powers opencode; runs under Bun). The Dock reuses the shared agentProjection folds (agentBucket / agentUrgency / agentPaintClass), so kolu-tui is a new renderer of the Dock-fleet-mirror contract, not a re-derivation. Built remote-FIRST as the de-risk milestone — local+remote PTY, reconnect, boot re-dial-re-adopt, adoption, the composite TerminalKey across two hosts, basic awareness — and it MUST exercise the pulam dial (awareness + fs/git ride one getHostSession(binary pulam) surface, so proving awareness proves fs/git's transport for free). It stops at the Dock + terminal + basic awareness — no Code tab, byte preview, paste/upload or transcript (full-client surface, deferred to F-REMOTE). Ships in remote-first phases K1-K3, after the substrate.

Contents

  • User-facing — kolu’s Dock, in the terminal
  • Architecture — embed the substrate · render with OpenTUI · reuse the Dock’s folds
  • What it proves vs skips
  • Implementation & phases — OpenTUI/Bun, the agentProjection reuse, K1–K3
  • See also

kolu itself, in the terminal. kolu already has two raw single-daemon CLIs — kaval-tui reads PTY bytes, pulam-tui reads awareness — and each is a scriptable face of one daemon. kolu-tui is a different animal: it embeds the terminal substrate (own process, own state — like kaval-tui is its own process, not a thin client of a running kolu-server) and lays out kolu’s own two-pane shape — the Dock rail on the left, the live terminal you type into as the main panel — as a full-screen TUI on OpenTUI. It’s the second consumer that proves the substrate graduated, and it’s built remote-first as the de-risk milestone for the finale.

User-facing — kolu’s Dock, in the terminal

The layout is kolu’s own, in the terminal: the Dock rail on the left — every terminal you own, grouped by host, each with its live agent badge — and the main panel is the live terminal you type into. The same two-pane shape as kolu-web (Dock + canvas), rendered by OpenTUI. You don’t leave one view to reach the other; both are on screen at once.

kolu-tuiOpenTUI · embeds @kolu/sutra
◧ DOCK
local
◜kolu · codex
✓notes · claude
ssh nix@prod
●kolu · claude
○infra · vim
ssh nix@prod · ~/kolu · feat/dial-ssh · claude ● awaiting you
⏺ Bash(cargo test)
⎿ test result: ok. 128 passed; 0 failed
I've got the ssh dial passing. Want me to wire the
reconnect backoff next, or open the PR first?
> open the PR 
↑↓ switch · ⇥ focus rail/term · c create · type → main pane · ● nix@prod/kolu awaiting you

You switch between terminals from the rail: ↑/↓ (or j/k) moves the selection and the main pane swaps to that terminal; ⇥ toggles focus between navigating the rail and typing into the terminal, so keystrokes go where you mean. The host headers make it kinda like tmux sessions — except a session can live on an ssh remote, and the rail’s badge tells you which terminal is awaiting you while you work in another. A detached terminal keeps running on its host; the rail just shows it dimmer.

The badges are not a re-implementation: they are the shared agentProjection folds the web Dock and pulam-web already use — agentBucket (working · awaiting · waiting), with the order≠colour split intact (a just-finished waiting agent ranks idle but paints awaiting). kolu-tui is a new renderer of the Dock ⇄ fleet-mirror contract, importing the folds, never re-deriving a switch over agent states.

Typing is raw passthrough — and it must not steal the inner agent's chords

When the main pane has focus, keystrokes are forwarded to that PTY raw (kolu-tui reuses kaval-tui’s attach machine — the escape machine + reply filter + terminal restore, via @kolu/terminal-protocol). kolu-tui owns only the rail + its own chrome; the terminal pane is total passthrough. So its focus/switch chords must respect input/prohibitedKeybinds.ts — it never claims a chord an inner tool needs (Claude Code’s Ctrl+B/Ctrl+J); the rail-focus toggle and the ssh-style line-start escape stay clear of them, exactly the reserved-keybinding rule the web client already follows.

Create on a remote host — c, pick a host; it’s the substrate’s create(location), one keystroke and one code path, and the new terminal lands in the rail focused in the main pane:

kolu-tui · c → create
host? nix@prod cmd? claude
# dialing nix@prod · kaval (PTY) + pulam (awareness) over ssh…
✓ added to the Dock · ssh nix@prod · ~/kolu · a3f9…c21 (survives the link)

What it deliberately is NOT — the scope line that keeps it honest

kolu-tui is the Dock rail + the terminal pane + basic awareness. It has no Code-tab fs/git browser, no binary/image preview, no paste/upload, no transcript export, no recency clock of its own (recency is kolu’s remembered fact, absent from the memoryless snapshot a dial serves). Those are full-client surface work that lands in F-REMOTE, not here. The smaller surface is the point: prove the hard transport/lifecycle volatility without the web rendering riding along.

Architecture — embed the substrate · render with OpenTUI · reuse the Dock’s folds

kolu-tui is its own process, composed of three parts that map cleanly onto kolu’s existing boundaries:

  1. The embedded substrate owns the lifecycle — create(location), attach(key), forEachHost at boot — exactly as kolu-server uses it. This is Option B and it is load-bearing: if kolu-tui were a thin renderer of a running kolu-server, nothing would graduate and it would be a second UI, not a second consumer. Because it embeds and dials, it is the proof.
  2. OpenTUI renders the two-pane layout — specifically its SolidJS reconciler @opentui/solid (compile-time JSX → renderables, no virtual DOM, the same Solid model kolu’s web client already uses), so the Dock rail and the terminal pane can be expressed in the framework kolu is built in. OpenTUI is the right reuse: it’s the SolidJS-native TUI framework (the lib that powers opencode), and pulam-tui already road-tested it in-repo.
  3. The shared agentProjection folds give the rail its meaning — agentBucket / agentUrgency / agentPaintClass from @kolu/terminal-workspace, imported (never re-derived), making kolu-tui the next renderer of the Dock ⇄ fleet-mirror contract.

The pulam-dial requirement — the proof condition, not an afterthought

kolu-tui must dial the host’s pulam for the rail’s awareness badges, and that is deliberate. Awareness and fs/git are procedures on the same terminalWorkspaceSurface, served by the same pulam binary, reached through the same getHostSession({ host, binary: "pulam" }) pool key (packages/surface-nix-host/src/hostSession.ts:911-929; pulam-tui dials it the same way, packages/pulam-tui/src/hostConnect.ts:56-64). So a kolu-tui that exercises the pulam dial for the Dock has by construction exercised the exact transport fs/git rides — same ssh provision, same pulam --stdio seam, same typed link. Even with no Code tab, kolu-tui proves the fs/git transport; what F-REMOTE adds is the rendering. Skip the pulam dial (e.g. fold only the in-process local producer) and the TUI stops being a de-risk — it becomes a second UI. This condition is what makes the milestone worth more than a product.

The three terminal-side clients, kept distinct:

kaval-tui pulam-tui kolu-tui
Dials kaval, binary: kaval — ptyHostSurface pulam, binary: pulam — terminalWorkspaceSurface embeds the substrate, which dials both
Sees raw PTY bytes derived meaning (agent/git/PR), no bytes a live terminal pane and the Dock rail
Renderer raw passthrough (Node) scriptable text/NDJSON (Node, no OpenTUI) full-screen OpenTUI two-pane (@opentui/solid, Bun)
Proves PTY transport over ssh awareness producer over ssh the substrate orchestration + both dials, end-to-end

pulam-tui shed OpenTUI; kolu-tui is where it earns its place

pulam-tui was a full OpenTUI dashboard, then reverted to a thin Node CLI (#1582) once pulam-web became the browser fleet board — a raw status/watch snapshot has no place for a full-screen renderer. kolu-tui is the opposite case: a two-pane Dock+terminal you switch and type in genuinely needs a layout/widget/reactivity engine, and hand-rolling one would violate the repo’s “prefer external libraries” rule. So OpenTUI comes back here, where it’s load-bearing rather than redundant.

What it proves vs skips

The milestone is honest only if the path it exists to prove is the one a done-criterion can only meet by hitting it — so name it exactly.

Proves (transfers verbatim to the full client):

  • The sealed substrate end-to-end — every op routed by TerminalKey, no host-seam bypass possible.
  • Remote PTY into the terminal pane over the kaval dial; reconnect / blip survival (HostSession backoff + the durable kaval --stdio daemon outliving the link).
  • Boot re-dial-re-adopt and adoption across hosts — forEachHost + the branded complete-host sweep, exercised on a real second host.
  • The composite TerminalKey under genuine pressure: two hosts whose bare PTY ids can collide, kept distinct by {host, ptyId}.
  • The rail fed over the pulam dial — which, per the condition above, proves the awareness and fs/git transport the full client inherits, and exercises the shared agentProjection as a TUI renderer.

Skips (deliberately — full-client surface, lands in F-REMOTE): the fs/git Code tab UI, the binary-preview byte route, paste/upload, transcript export, rich awareness rendering + restore UX. kaval-tui already proves raw PTY; kolu-tui’s added value is the kolu orchestration, the two-pane Dock+terminal, and the dial coverage — never the rendering F-REMOTE owns.

The de-risk, in one line

kolu-tui lights the hardest, least-reversible third of remote terminals — transport, reconnect, host-lifecycle, adoption, the composite key — behind kolu’s real Dock, with no web rendering, and ships as a usable product while doing it. The full client then inherits a proven substrate and only has to add its own pixels.

Implementation & phases — OpenTUI/Bun, the agentProjection reuse, K1–K3

The library decisions are made (so an implementer doesn’t have to guess):

  • Renderer: OpenTUI via @opentui/solid. SolidJS reconciler, so the Dock rail and terminal pane are authored in the same framework as the web client; reuse the presentation-neutral agentProjection folds outright, and reuse the web Dock’s component logic where it’s portable (the structure + the folds port; DOM/CSS specifics do not — confirm the component-reuse depth at K1).
  • Runtime: Bun — OpenTUI’s native Zig core needs it (the same reason pulam-tui ran under Bun before reverting). The substrate dials kaval/pulam daemons over sockets/ssh rather than spawning native PTYs in-process, so the Bun process carries no native PTY dependency; the kaval/pulam daemons stay as they are.
  • Honest caveat: OpenTUI is pre-1.0 (“not ready for production” upstream). It’s the right SolidJS-native bet and has in-repo precedent, but pin a version and treat a renderer-API break as a real K1 risk — the substrate/awareness work behind the Dock is unaffected either way.

Three increments, each shippable, each remote-from-the-start. They come after the substrate extraction.

kolu-tui — remote-first, on the embedded substrate✓ shipped · ▶ do next · ◐ build-clean · ○ todo · → drill in
○K1 · embed + the Dock — link @kolu/sutra; render the two-pane Dock rail + terminal on OpenTUI (@opentui/solid); switch/type across local AND ssh hosts; the create knobneeds SUBSTRATE
○K2 · survive the link — reconnect + blip survival · boot re-dial-re-adopt · adoption across hosts · composite TerminalKey proven on ≥2 hostsneeds K1
○K3 · live rail badges over the pulam dial — agentProjection (working/awaiting/waiting) per row, proving the awareness + fs/git transportneeds K2 · the proof condition
○→ F-REMOTE · the full web tile inherits the proven substrate + both dials→ finale
  • K1 — embed + the Dock. The skeleton that proves Option B: kolu-tui embeds the substrate (own process, under Bun) and renders the two-pane Dock rail + terminal on OpenTUI; create/kill, switch-between-terminals (rail selection swaps the main pane), and raw typing into the focused pane work across local and ssh hosts through the kaval dial. Reuses kaval-tui’s raw attach machine (the escape machine + reply filter + restore, via @kolu/terminal-protocol). Done: a remote terminal appears in the rail, is selected/typed-into/killed through the substrate; the PTY survives a detach.
  • K2 — survive the link. Reconnect, boot re-dial-re-adopt, adoption — the substrate’s hard volatility under real ssh conditions. Done: kill the link mid-session → the pane reconnects and the remote PTY is intact; restart kolu-tui → it re-dials each host and re-adopts surviving PTYs; two hosts with colliding bare ids stay distinct.
  • K3 — live rail badges over the pulam dial. Dial each host’s pulam, fold the snapshot stream through the shared agentProjection into each rail row’s bucketed badge + live ●. Done: a remote agent’s working → awaiting shows in the rail while you work in another pane — proving the pulam transport (and thus fs/git’s) that F-REMOTE’s Code tab will ride.
